title: Stuffed vine leaves
subtitle: Warak dawali, rice and herb filled rolls in lemony broth
excerpt: Preserved vine leaves filled with a mixture of long-grain rice, parsley, mint, and allspice, tightly rolled and packed into a pot with a weight on top, then simmered in water and lemon juice until the rice is cooked and the leaves are tender. A labour-intensive dish that rewards patience, the filling expands as the rice cooks, so the rolls must be tight.
sourceNotes: Stuffed vine leaves (warak dawali, warak inab, dolma) appear across the Levant, Turkey, Iran, and Greece. The Lebanese version documented in Anissa Helou's 'Lebanese Cuisine' (1994) uses rice with herbs and spices rather than meat. A meat-and-rice version also exists. Vine leaves preserved in brine are widely available in jars.

content.text: The rolls must be tight. Loose rolls fall apart as the rice expands during cooking, the filling spills out, and the whole pot becomes a mess. Each vine leaf should be rolled firmly around the filling with the ends tucked under, like a cigar. When packed into the pot, the rolls should sit snugly against each other so they cannot unravel. A heatproof plate placed directly on top of the rolls keeps them in place during cooking.

Use preserved vine leaves from a jar. Rinse them well under cold water to remove the brine and separate them gently. They tear easily. Any leaves that are too small or badly torn can be laid flat on the base of the pot to prevent the rolls from catching. Serve the stuffed leaves with lemon wedges and plain yoghurt.

items.name: Vine leaves
items.prepNote: from a jar, drained and rinsed

content.text: Combine the {{long-grain-rice}}, grated {{onion}}, {{parsley-flat}}, {{mint}}, {{tomato}}, {{allspice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}, and salt in a bowl. Mix well. Lay a rinsed {{vine-leaves}} leaf smooth-side down on a board. Place a heaped teaspoon of filling near the stem end. Fold in the sides, then roll tightly from stem to tip. Repeat with remaining leaves and filling.

Line the base of a large saucepan with any remaining vine leaves or tomato slices. Arrange the rolls seam-side down in tight layers. Pour over the {{lemon}} juice and enough water to just cover (about 400 ml). Place a heatproof plate directly on top of the rolls to weigh them down. Bring to the boil, reduce to a low simmer, cover, and cook for 45 to 55 minutes until the rice is cooked through. Rest for 10 minutes before lifting out carefully.

items.fix: Each roll needs firm pressure when rolled and the pot needs to be fully packed so the rolls hold each other in place. The plate on top is essential
items.cause: Not rolled tightly enough or packed too loosely

items.fix: Check the liquid level at 30 minutes, add boiling water if the rolls are sitting dry. The liquid should be actively simmering, not just steaming
items.cause: Too little liquid or heat too low
items.symptom: Rice is still firm after 55 minutes

items.fix: Rinse jarred leaves thoroughly under cold water. They need the full cooking time, 45 minutes minimum. Older leaves in a jar may need an extra 10 to 15 minutes
items.cause: Leaves not rinsed or cooked long enough

items.fix: Taste the filling before rolling. It should be well seasoned since the flavour dilutes into the cooking liquid. Add extra allspice, salt, and lemon if needed
items.cause: Insufficient seasoning in the raw mix

content.text: Stuffed vine leaves are one of the most widespread dishes in the Eastern Mediterranean and the Middle East, appearing in Lebanese, Syrian, Turkish, Greek, Iranian, and Egyptian cuisines with variations in filling. The Lebanese vegetarian version with rice and herbs is a staple of mezze spreads and family cooking. The Turkish and Iranian versions often include meat. The common thread is the technique: filling that expands as it cooks, held in by a leaf that softens and becomes a flavour carrier. In Lebanon, warak dawali appear at most large family gatherings, made in quantity and served warm or at room temperature.
